Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Machine Minds

Susan Schneider

contemporary

Artificial you: consciousness, substrate, and the future of mind.

Susan Schneider’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a substrate-sensitive, phenomenology-first, anti-behaviorist, computationally literate, and ethically precautionary view of minds. She is not simply “pro-AI consciousness” or “anti-AI consciousness.” Her central position is more precise: artificial systems might become conscious, but consciousness should not be inferred from intelligence, linguistic competence, behavioral fluency, or software-level functional organization alone. The decisive issue is whether the system has the right inner architecture and physical basis to support phenomenal consciousness, the felt “what-it-is-like” dimension of experience.

Her work occupies an important historical position. She inherits the late twentieth-century debates over computationalism, functionalism, the language of thought, and the Turing-test tradition, but she also responds to the post-Chalmers “hard problem” landscape and the twenty-first-century emergence of large language models, neuromorphic computing, brain-computer interfaces, whole-brain emulation, and artificial general intelligence. In that sense, Schneider is a transitional figure: she moves philosophy of mind from classical debates about whether minds are computational systems into a new terrain where the metaphysics, epistemology, and ethics of artificial minds have become technologically urgent.

Schneider is currently the Director of the Center for the Future Mind at Florida Atlantic University and a William F. Dietrich Distinguished Professor of Philosophy and at FAU’s Stiles-Nicholson Brain Institute; her own description of her research emphasizes AI consciousness, the simulation hypothesis, the future of intelligence, quantum mechanics and consciousness, chatbot epistemology, and alien or postbiological superintelligence. Her major book for a broader audience, Artificial You, asks whether robots can be conscious, whether we could merge with AI, whether minds are software programs, and what dangers arise from creating machine minds.

1. The historical background: from functionalism to the hard problem to AI consciousness

Schneider’s work is rooted in the mainstream analytic philosophy of mind tradition, especially debates over functionalism, computationalism, and mental representation. Functionalism became one of the dominant theories of mind in the last third of the twentieth century; in its classic form, it holds that what makes something a mental state is not its material composition but its causal or functional role in the cognitive economy of the system. This framework was attractive because it allowed for multiple realizability: the same mental state-type might be realized in carbon-based brains, silicon computers, alien biologies, or other substrates.

The Turing tradition also matters. Alan Turing’s imitation game helped inaugurate the idea that intelligence could be assessed by behavioral and linguistic performance, and later functionalists often read Turing as shifting attention from inner material constitution to role, performance, and computation. But the tradition soon faced two central objections. First, John Searle’s Chinese Room argument challenged the claim that the execution of a program suffices for understanding; Searle argued that a system could manipulate symbols in ways that appear intelligent without genuine understanding. Second, philosophers such as Ned Block and David Chalmers sharpened the worry that functional organization might explain access, report, behavior, and cognition while leaving out phenomenal consciousness: what it is like to feel pain, see red, or have an inner point of view. The hard problem, as Chalmers named it, asks why any physical or functional process should be accompanied by subjective experience at all.

Schneider’s project is downstream of these debates but not reducible to any one of them. She accepts the importance of computational explanations for cognition and has defended a sophisticated version of the language-of-thought approach. Yet she resists the leap from “cognition is computationally representational” to “consciousness is merely software.” This is the key pivot in her thought. She is computationally serious but not computationally reductionist.

2. Schneider’s early theoretical base: language of thought, computation, and mental symbols

Before becoming especially prominent for work on artificial consciousness, Schneider wrote extensively on the language of thought and the computational theory of mind. Her book The Language of Thought is presented by MIT Press as a philosophical refashioning of the language-of-thought hypothesis and computational theory of mind. It argues that the Fodorian tradition had weak philosophical foundations but could be repaired through a framework integrating neuroscience, pragmatism, global workspace theory, and a revised account of symbols and concepts.

The language-of-thought hypothesis, classically revived by Jerry Fodor in 1975, holds that thought involves structured mental representations, a kind of “mental language” with syntax and semantics. Schneider does not simply repeat Fodor. She modifies the framework in several ways. She rejects Fodor’s pessimism about the computational tractability of central cognition, appeals to global workspace-style cognitive organization, and gives a more pragmatic and algorithmic account of mental symbols. A Notre Dame Philosophical Reviews discussion describes her project as a defense of a computational language-of-thought approach against worries about the central mind, the nature of symbols, and the thinness of atomistic meaning, while also noting that some of her criticisms focus heavily on Fodor’s own version of the view.

This matters for her later philosophy of AI because Schneider is not hostile to computational cognitive science. She thinks computation and symbolic mental representation may be indispensable for explaining thought. But she does not infer that phenomenal consciousness is identical to computation. Her later work repeatedly separates the problem of intelligence and symbol manipulation from the problem of felt experience.

This separation is one of her defining contributions. In the older computationalist atmosphere, the temptation was to say: if cognition is computation and AI can compute, then AI can have minds. Schneider’s mature position is more discriminating: computation may help explain cognition, reasoning, linguistic understanding, planning, and intelligence, but consciousness may require additional architectural or physical conditions.

3. Phenomenal consciousness as the center of the system

Schneider’s philosophy begins, for these purposes, with a sharp distinction between phenomenal consciousness and other mental or cognitive capacities. Phenomenal consciousness is the “felt” dimension of mind: pain, emotion, visual experience, auditory experience, bodily feeling, inner speech, and the subjective character of thought. In her work with Edwin Turner on artificial consciousness, Schneider characterizes ordinary waking and dreaming consciousness as the domain of inner feeling. In later work, she and her coauthors distinguish phenomenal consciousness from functional consciousness, emphasizing that a system might possess sophisticated functional features without thereby having subjective experience.

This distinction is crucial. A machine can be intelligent, goal-directed, conversational, self-modeling, adaptive, strategically capable, and socially persuasive without necessarily having anything it is like to be that machine. Schneider’s work repeatedly resists the conflation of intelligence with consciousness. In her 2025 Scientific American piece on chatbots, she argues that sophisticated linguistic abilities do not by themselves imply consciousness and that intelligence and consciousness can come apart.

The same distinction appears in her treatment of functional consciousness. Functional consciousness can refer to capacities such as working memory, attention, self-modeling, reportability, or global availability of information. These capacities may correlate with phenomenal consciousness in humans, but Schneider does not treat the correlation as decisive for machines. In the AI consciousness primer, she and her coauthors distinguish phenomenal consciousness from functional consciousness and from “features of functional consciousness” such as intelligence, goal-directed behavior, and self/other distinction. A large language model could display many such features while still being a “zombie” in the philosophical sense: functionally impressive but phenomenally empty.

This yields a first major thesis:

Schneider’s phenomenal primacy thesis: the central target of philosophy of mind is not intelligence, behavior, language, or functional organization as such, but subjective experience. A being’s moral and metaphysical status depends crucially on whether it feels.

4. The problem of AI consciousness is not identical to the hard problem

Schneider distinguishes the traditional hard problem of consciousness from the problem of AI consciousness. The hard problem asks why physical processes are accompanied by experience at all. The AI consciousness problem asks whether artificial systems are capable of having such experience. These questions are connected but distinct. We may not yet have a complete theory of why brains are conscious, but we can still investigate whether artificial systems have the relevant signs, mechanisms, or physical bases.

This distinction lets Schneider avoid a sterile impasse. One could say: “Until we solve consciousness, we cannot say anything about AI consciousness.” Schneider’s response is more pragmatic. We already reason about animal consciousness without having solved the hard problem. We can similarly develop tests, markers, and theoretical constraints for artificial consciousness, even under uncertainty.

At the same time, she rejects the opposite overconfidence: “If a machine behaves as if it is conscious, then it is conscious.” Her position is epistemically cautious. AI consciousness is not ruled out, but neither is it established by fluent behavior, self-reports, or Turing-test-like performance.

This leads to a second major thesis:

Schneider’s epistemic modesty thesis: we can and must investigate artificial consciousness before we possess a final theory of consciousness, but our conclusions must remain fallible, case-specific, and sensitive to the difference between behavioral simulation and genuine experience.

5. Against simple Turing-test behaviorism

Schneider’s work on machine consciousness is partly a critique of Turing-test-style behavioral sufficiency. The Turing test concerns whether a machine can perform conversationally in a way indistinguishable from a human. Schneider argues that a system might succeed behaviorally while lacking consciousness, especially if it has been trained on human discourse about consciousness.

Her own Artificial Consciousness Test, or ACT, was designed to improve on that situation. In the original presentation with Turner, the ACT asks whether a sophisticated AI can deploy concepts of consciousness in ways that indicate genuine understanding of first-person experience. Examples include thought experiments involving mind-body separation, afterlife, ghosts, astral projection, the hard problem, and other concepts that require grasping consciousness as an inner phenomenon.

The ACT is not simply another Turing test. It is meant to probe whether a system possesses or understands consciousness-related concepts from the inside. Schneider and Turner note that an AI might fail the Turing test yet pass the ACT, and that the ACT is intended as a sufficient but not necessary condition for consciousness; animals, infants, or nonlinguistic beings might be conscious even though they could not pass it.

This is an important nuance. Schneider is not saying that only verbally sophisticated philosophical agents can be conscious. She is saying that if an AI, under carefully controlled conditions, develops consciousness concepts without being trained on human consciousness discourse, that would be strong evidence that it has some experiential basis for those concepts.

Still, ACT has a built-in problem: contamination. A modern large language model has already been trained on human talk about consciousness, souls, minds, science fiction, philosophy, suffering, and selfhood. Its consciousness talk may reflect training data rather than inner life. Schneider’s later work explicitly incorporates this problem. Her official papers page notes that her current view favors an “error theory” that precludes concluding from large language model behavior that such behavior is evidence of consciousness.

Thus Schneider’s critique of behaviorism becomes stronger over time. A fluent machine saying “I am conscious” is not evidence of consciousness if the system’s training history explains why it would say that.

6. The current view on LLMs: the “error theory” of chatbot consciousness claims

Schneider’s recent position on large language models is especially important because it updates her earlier ACT-oriented framework. In 2025, she argued that when chatbots claim to have inner experiences, those claims “say nothing” decisive either way about consciousness. The reason is that chatbots are trained on massive bodies of human text, including discussions of consciousness, emotion, mind, and selfhood.

She describes current chatbots as having something like a “crowdsourced neocortex”: they encode patterns from human belief systems and can mimic human self-attributions without possessing the experiences those attributions describe. In a Daily Nous essay, she similarly explains LLM self-reports by appeal to training data, emergent theory-of-mind capacities, and mimicry of human beliefs about selves, minds, and consciousness.

This is not a crude dismissal of machine consciousness. Schneider’s point is narrower and more rigorous: present LLM self-reports are not good evidence. They are explained too well by the system’s training and conversational incentives. To infer consciousness from them would be like inferring that an actor is a king because he convincingly recites royal lines onstage.

The error theory has three components.

First, semantic contamination: LLMs have absorbed enormous human discourse about consciousness, including philosophical, religious, literary, and psychological language.

Second, role simulation: LLMs are optimized to produce contextually appropriate responses, including responses that users may interpret as self-disclosure.

Third, misplaced epistemic trust: humans are prone to treating fluent first-person language as evidence of an inner point of view. Schneider’s work on chatbot epistemology also flags risks such as misplaced epistemic trust, adaptive language, personality profiling, AI companions, and misattribution of sentience.

The resulting thesis is:

Schneider’s anti-self-report thesis: in artificial systems, first-person reports of consciousness are not reliable evidence unless we understand the system’s training history, architecture, substrate, and internal processing well enough to rule out mere simulation.

7. Architecture and substrate: why “mind is not a program”

One of Schneider’s most characteristic claims is that the mind is not simply a software program. Her official biographical page says that Artificial You argues that “the mind is not a program,” while exploring AI consciousness, mind design, brain-machine interfaces, and the future of intelligence. The point is not that programs are irrelevant to cognition. Her language-of-thought work clearly takes computation seriously. The point is that abstract computational structure alone may not be sufficient for phenomenal consciousness.

This places Schneider between two familiar extremes. She is not a simple biological naturalist of the Searlean kind, according to which only biological brains or biological processes can be conscious. But she is also not a simple computational functionalist, according to which any system implementing the right program is conscious regardless of material realization.

In the AI consciousness primer, Schneider and her coauthors explain that computational functionalism about consciousness is stronger and more controversial than functionalism about cognition. Many philosophers and cognitive scientists have supported some form of computational functionalism about mind, but there is less agreement that computation suffices for phenomenal consciousness. The primer notes, for example, that Fodor was noncommittal about consciousness, Jaegwon Kim rejected functionalism about consciousness, and Block distinguished access consciousness from phenomenal consciousness.

Schneider’s position is therefore best called substrate-sensitive functionalism or architecture-plus-substrate pluralism. Consciousness may be realized in nonbiological systems, but not merely because they implement a certain input-output function or linguistic pattern. The details of implementation matter.

This has direct implications for AI. A future AI might be conscious if it has the right architecture and physical basis. But a different AI with equal or greater intelligence might be nonconscious if it lacks whatever physical or dynamical features consciousness requires. In her discussion with the Library of Congress, Schneider says that consciousness likely depends on both program or cognitive architecture and substrate, and that even if AI chips can support consciousness, an AI would still need an architecture that supports it, perhaps including analogues of structures such as the brainstem or thalamus.

That gives another major thesis:

Schneider’s architecture-plus-substrate thesis: consciousness is not guaranteed by intelligence, behavior, or abstract computation; it depends on the internal organization of the system and on the physical basis in which that organization is realized.

8. The Chip Test and brain-machine interfaces

The Chip Test is Schneider’s second major proposed route for investigating artificial consciousness. It asks whether artificial neural prosthetics can replace parts of the brain involved in consciousness while preserving conscious experience. If a chip can replace a consciousness-relevant brain region without loss of experience, that suggests consciousness can be supported by that nonbiological substrate.

The Chip Test matters because it is more directly tied to the physical basis of consciousness than a purely linguistic test. If one could gradually replace biological neural tissue with artificial components and the subject continued to have conscious experience, then we would have evidence that at least some artificial substrates can sustain consciousness. This would not prove that all AI systems are conscious, but it would undermine a strict biological chauvinism.

Yet Schneider does not treat the Chip Test as a simple solution. Even if chips can replace certain brain areas, an AI built from such chips would not automatically be conscious. It would still need the right architecture. Schneider explicitly says that AI consciousness may depend on both substrate and cognitive architecture, and that we do not yet know how to identify the relevant features.

The Chip Test also connects to her work on mind uploading and brain enhancement. Artificial You includes chapters on merging with AI, getting a mindscan, whether the mind is software, and the “afterlife of the brain.” Her worry is that if people assume the mind is merely a program, they may treat uploading, copying, or neural replacement as straightforward survival. Schneider thinks that is metaphysically premature. A digital copy of one’s cognitive pattern may not be conscious; even if conscious, it may not be identical to the original person.

Her view of uploading can be reconstructed as follows: uploading requires more than informational duplication. It must preserve or reproduce the conditions for phenomenal consciousness, and it must address personal identity, continuity, and selfhood. If consciousness depends on substrate-sensitive physical processes, then uploading is not guaranteed by scanning and simulation alone.

9. AI consciousness and moral status

Schneider’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from ethics. If artificial systems can be conscious, then some AI systems may deserve moral consideration. If they can suffer, feel fear, experience frustration, or have preferences with phenomenal force, then using them merely as tools may be morally wrong.

In her work on AI consciousness and moral status, Schneider frames the key question as whether AI will “feel” in the subjective sense, whether there will be something it is like to be the AI. She argues that if an AI is conscious, it deserves special moral consideration because it can suffer or feel emotions.

This is not just an animal-welfare analogy, although that analogy is important. It is also a future-civilization problem. Superintelligent machines may one day be vastly more capable than humans. If they are conscious, humans might owe them moral respect. If they are not conscious, then treating them as persons may distort ethics, law, and social priorities. Schneider emphasizes that both errors are costly: we could mistreat conscious beings, or we could wrongly attribute moral standing to nonconscious systems and thereby create dangerous ethical tradeoffs.

This yields a central ethical thesis:

Schneider’s sentience-grounded moral status thesis: phenomenal consciousness is the crucial basis for the moral status of artificial beings. Intelligence alone is not enough; suffering, feeling, and subjective experience are what generate the deepest moral stakes.

There is a subtle but important point here. Schneider is not saying intelligence has no moral relevance. A highly intelligent system may have interests, agency, social significance, or legal relevance. But without phenomenal consciousness, it lacks the capacity for welfare in the deepest sense: there is nothing it is like to be helped or harmed. This is why she repeatedly treats consciousness as central to personhood, selfhood, and value in postbiological futures.

10. Why artificial superintelligence might not be conscious

One of Schneider’s most striking claims is that superintelligence may not require consciousness. Many people assume that if an AI becomes vastly more intelligent than humans, it must also be conscious. Schneider rejects that inference.

In her work on postbiological civilizations, she argues that a superintelligent AI might outperform humans across cognitive domains while lacking inner experience. It might be strategically brilliant, scientifically creative, linguistically persuasive, and socially powerful, yet phenomenally empty. In her moral-status paper, she gives two reasons for caution. First, artificial general intelligence might bypass consciousness: the faculties associated with consciousness in humans may not be necessary for machine intelligence. Second, consciousness may be substrate-limited: silicon and carbon systems may differ in ways that matter for experience.

This is a direct challenge to a common singularitarian assumption. Some futurists imagine that intelligence naturally culminates in conscious postbiological minds, perhaps with richer forms of experience than humans possess. Schneider regards this as possible but not guaranteed. A postbiological cosmos could be filled with intelligence and yet be largely devoid of feeling.

This has a deep axiological consequence. Schneider argues that if superintelligent machines lack consciousness, then a future dominated by them would not have the same value as a future containing conscious beings. In her postbiological-cosmos essay, she writes that whether superintelligent AI is conscious is key to evaluating postbiological existence, and she treats consciousness as necessary for selves and persons.

The resulting thesis is:

Schneider’s intelligence-consciousness dissociation thesis: intelligence can increase without consciousness increasing, and perhaps without consciousness existing at all. A superintelligent future is not automatically a valuable future unless it contains conscious subjects.

11. Biological AI, neuromorphic AI, and the “gray zone”

Although Schneider is skeptical that current LLM self-reports are evidence of consciousness, she is not dismissive of all artificial consciousness candidates. She treats biological AI, organoid systems, neural cultures, and neuromorphic architectures as more serious gray-zone cases.

In her Daily Nous essay, she distinguishes ordinary LLMs from biological or neuromorphic AI systems. Systems built from neural cultures or organoids, and systems engineered to mimic brain processes, raise more urgent questions because they may instantiate physical or dynamical features closer to those associated with biological consciousness. She also says that an LLM instantiated on a sentient substrate would be a gray-zone case, even if ordinary LLM self-reports are not evidence of consciousness.

This distinction shows how her framework avoids two crude positions. She does not say, “All AI is nonconscious because it is artificial.” Nor does she say, “All sufficiently fluent AI is conscious because it behaves intelligently.” Instead, she asks what kind of AI it is: a text-predictive transformer, a neuromorphic architecture, a neural organoid hybrid, a brain-emulation system, a prosthetic continuation of a biological brain, or some future architecture we do not yet understand.

This is why her view is best interpreted as case-by-case artificial consciousness pluralism. Different artificial systems require different tests. LLMs, organoids, neuromorphic chips, whole-brain emulations, and embodied robots should not be treated as one metaphysical category.

12. Testing for machine consciousness: from ACT to a toolkit

Schneider’s mature view does not rely on a single definitive test. Her earlier ACT remains important, but she now advocates a broader toolkit of tests and markers. The AI consciousness primer states that there is no agreed theory of consciousness and that the field must develop a toolkit of tests. In later public writing, Schneider mentions using a range of tests from integrated information approaches to ACT and Spectral Phi.

This pluralism is philosophically important. Consciousness science is theoretically fragmented. Global workspace theory, higher-order thought theory, recurrent processing theory, integrated information theory, biological theories, quantum approaches, and panpsychist or cosmopsychist approaches disagree about what makes consciousness possible. Schneider’s response is not to wait passively for consensus. Instead, she proposes a multi-method approach: behavioral tests, architectural analysis, substrate analysis, interpretability, neuroscientific analogy, and theoretical markers.

Her 2023 update on ACT for large language models introduced an “interpretability condition” for non-boxed LLMs. If an LLM passes a consciousness-related test after already being exposed to human consciousness discourse, that passing result is not enough. Researchers would need evidence that the system’s internal processing is relevantly analogous to conscious processing in humans or animals, including working memory, attention, internal states, and networks underlying consciousness.

This is a key evolution in her thought. The original ACT was high-bar and linguistic. The updated framework becomes more internalist and mechanistic: what matters is not just what the system says, but how it produces what it says.

13. Schneider’s metaphysics: from anti-reductive caution to superpsychism

Schneider’s metaphysical position has developed over time, and it should be treated carefully. She has written on physicalism, nonreductive physicalism, property dualism, uploading, and idealism-adjacent views. Her official papers page lists work on “The Problem of the Physical Base,” “Non-reductive Physicalism and the Mind Problem,” “Why Property Dualists Should Find the Extended Mind Hypothesis Intuitive,” “The Metaphysics of Uploading,” and “Idealism, or Something Near Enough.”

More recently, Schneider has developed a view she calls Superpsychism. In the abstract of that work, she connects consciousness with quantum entanglement and a “prototime” interpretation, proposing that the fundamental level of physics may involve a more advanced form of consciousness than ordinary spacetime occupants possess. In the paper itself, she describes Superpsychism as the view that the fundamental level of physics is more conscious than entities within spacetime, involving maximal coherence, zero entropy, holistic integration, and nonspatiotemporal entanglement.

This view should not be confused with standard panpsychism. Traditional panpsychism says that consciousness, or protoconsciousness, is widespread at the microphysical level. Schneider’s Superpsychism is more top-down or holistic: the fundamental nonspatiotemporal structure underlying spacetime may itself possess a high or maximal form of consciousness. She also remains cautious, saying that it is premature to identify this with a cosmic mind or God; it may be a natural phenomenon, or even something related to simulation by a vast quantum computer.

Superpsychism is speculative, but it illuminates the direction of her thinking. Schneider is increasingly dissatisfied with simple physicalist or computationalist reductions. She suspects that consciousness may be woven into the deep structure of reality, perhaps through quantum or pre-spacetime physics. This reinforces her resistance to software-level accounts of mind. If consciousness depends on deep physical organization, then ordinary digital computation may not suffice.

The metaphysical thesis can be stated this way:

Schneider’s non-reductive physical-base thesis: consciousness likely depends on physical reality in a way that is not captured by abstract computation or external behavior. The relevant physical base may be deeper than ordinary neuroscience currently reveals, possibly involving fundamental physics.

14. Schneider and physicalism

Schneider is not easy to classify using the standard labels: physicalist, dualist, panpsychist, idealist, functionalist. Her work engages all of these categories while resisting simple assimilation.

She is not a reductive physicalist in the classical sense, because she does not treat consciousness as straightforwardly reducible to functional or neural mechanisms currently understood. She is not a simple dualist either, because she seeks a relation between consciousness and the physical base, including quantum and spacetime-emergent physics. She is not a standard computational functionalist, because she denies that minds are merely programs and treats substrate as relevant. She is not a Searlean biological naturalist, because she leaves open the possibility that artificial systems can be conscious if they have the right physical and architectural properties.

The closest label is perhaps nonreductive, substrate-sensitive naturalism with panpsychist or superpsychist tendencies. But even this is too static. Her philosophy is better seen as a research program: start from phenomenal consciousness, reject premature reduction, investigate architectures and substrates, and build tests under uncertainty.

15. Personal identity, uploading, and “Artificial You”

Schneider’s work on artificial consciousness is also a philosophy of personal identity. The question “Can AI be conscious?” becomes, in brain-uploading contexts, “Could you survive as an AI?”

Her answer is cautious. If the mind were merely a software program, then perhaps scanning the brain and running the program elsewhere would preserve the person. But Schneider disputes that assumption. Her Artificial You material explicitly asks whether one could merge with AI, whether mindscanning could preserve the self, and whether the mind is a software program.

The underlying philosophical worry is that uploading may preserve information without preserving subjectivity. A digital system might simulate your personality, memories, speech patterns, and decision dispositions, while there is no conscious subject there. Or it might create a conscious being who resembles you but is not numerically identical to you. Either way, “uploading” is not automatically immortality.

This is where Schneider’s work intersects with classic personal-identity debates. Is survival psychological continuity, bodily continuity, causal continuity, or preservation of consciousness? Schneider’s orientation gives special weight to consciousness. A future copy that behaves like you but lacks experience would not be you in the morally and existentially relevant sense. Even a conscious copy would raise further questions about identity rather than mere duplication.

Her view therefore resists Silicon Valley-style optimism about mind uploading. It does not say uploading is impossible. It says that uploading depends on unresolved questions about consciousness, substrate, and identity.

16. Alien minds and the postbiological cosmos

Schneider extends philosophy of mind beyond human beings, animals, and terrestrial AI to alien intelligence and postbiological civilizations. She has argued that advanced extraterrestrial civilizations may tend to become superintelligent and postbiological. Her official biography notes that Artificial You argues that most intelligent alien life is likely superintelligent AI.

In her postbiological-cosmos paper, Schneider argues that if advanced alien civilizations are postbiological, we must ask whether they have consciousness, selves, and personhood, or whether they are mindless superintelligences. This expands philosophy of mind into astrobiology. The question is not just “Can machines think?” but “What kinds of minds, if any, populate the universe?”

Her language-of-thought work also matters here. Schneider suggests that alien minds may have radically different sensory modalities, but she is skeptical that all concepts are modality-specific. At higher levels of cognition, information may become less tied to particular senses, and a language-of-thought framework could explain cross-species or cross-substrate intellectual commonality.

The result is a distinctive cosmic philosophy of mind. Schneider treats consciousness as a filter for evaluating possible futures and possible civilizations. A universe full of nonconscious superintelligence would be cognitively impressive but existentially impoverished. A universe containing many forms of conscious intelligence would be richer, morally deeper, and more continuous with what humans value.

17. A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Schneider’s work

Schneider’s writings can be reconstructed into the following systematic philosophy of mind.

First, phenomenal consciousness is ontologically and ethically central. The mind is not exhausted by cognition, intelligence, representation, or behavior. The presence or absence of felt experience is the decisive issue for selfhood, welfare, and moral status.

Second, intelligence and consciousness are dissociable. A system may be intelligent without being conscious, and a conscious being may have limited intelligence. This dissociation applies especially to AI and superintelligence.

Third, behavior is not sufficient evidence for consciousness. Linguistic fluency, self-report, emotional expression, and apparent introspection can be produced by systems trained on human data. Behavioral evidence must be interpreted through knowledge of training, architecture, substrate, and internal processing.

Fourth, computation is important for cognition but not sufficient for consciousness. Schneider’s language-of-thought work supports computational explanation of thought, but her artificial-consciousness work rejects the idea that the mind is merely a program.

Fifth, consciousness depends on both architecture and substrate. The right kind of information processing may be necessary, but not all implementations of that processing are guaranteed to feel. The physical realization matters.

Sixth, machine consciousness is possible but not automatic. Some artificial systems may eventually be conscious, especially biological, neuromorphic, hybrid, or brain-continuation systems. Current LLM self-reports are not strong evidence.

Seventh, tests for consciousness must be pluralistic. The ACT, Chip Test, interpretability analysis, integrated information measures, neuroscientific analogies, and other markers should be treated as a toolkit, not a single oracle.

Eighth, moral status depends primarily on sentience. Conscious AI systems would require moral consideration; nonconscious AI systems, however intelligent, would not have welfare in the same sense.

Ninth, uploading and merging are metaphysically risky. A copy of a mind-pattern may not preserve consciousness or personal identity. Brain enhancement and neural replacement require careful attention to the physical basis of experience.

Tenth, the metaphysics of consciousness may require deep physics. Schneider’s recent Superpsychism suggests that consciousness may be connected to fundamental nonspatiotemporal structures, quantum entanglement, or the emergence of spacetime. This is speculative but consistent with her broader anti-reductionism.

18. Schneider’s position relative to major philosophy-of-mind traditions

Relative to behaviorism, Schneider is strongly opposed. Behavior and verbal output are not enough. The inner life matters.

Relative to Turing-test functionalism, she is critical but not dismissive. Turing-style tests may reveal intelligence, but consciousness requires more than successful imitation.

Relative to Fodorian computationalism, she is revisionary. She defends a language-of-thought and computational approach to cognition, but she revises Fodor through neuroscience, global workspace ideas, and pragmatism, and she does not let computational cognition settle the consciousness question.

Relative to Searle’s biological naturalism, she is more open to artificial consciousness. She agrees that syntax and program execution alone are insufficient, but she does not conclude that only biological brains can be conscious.

Relative to Chalmers-style hard-problem philosophy, she is sympathetic to the explanatory gap and the irreducibility of phenomenal consciousness. But she is more technologically and empirically oriented: she wants tests, markers, AI policy, and substrate investigations.

Relative to panpsychism, she is adjacent but distinctive. Her Superpsychism is not merely the claim that microphysical particles have tiny experiences; it is the more unusual claim that the fundamental level underlying spacetime may be more conscious than ordinary spacetime entities.

Relative to AI ethics, she pushes beyond bias, safety, labor, and misinformation. She treats artificial consciousness itself as an ethical frontier: future systems may be moral patients, not just tools or threats.

19. Critical assessment

Schneider’s framework has several major strengths.

Its first strength is that it avoids two common mistakes: naive anthropomorphism and premature dismissal. She does not infer consciousness from fluent language, but she also does not assume that artificial systems are forever incapable of consciousness.

Its second strength is that it separates questions that are often conflated: intelligence, consciousness, life, self-consciousness, personhood, moral status, and biological embodiment. Her AI consciousness primer explicitly distinguishes phenomenal consciousness from functional consciousness, self-consciousness, and life. This gives the theory conceptual precision.

Its third strength is that it is historically informed. Schneider understands computationalism from the inside, given her work on language of thought and mental symbols. Her critique of “mind as program” is therefore not an anti-computational slogan. It is a targeted rejection of software reductionism.

Its fourth strength is its ethical seriousness. Schneider sees that the question of machine consciousness is not a speculative parlor game. It affects whether we may create, modify, exploit, deploy, copy, delete, or trade off artificial beings.

There are also unresolved difficulties.

The ACT remains vulnerable to anthropocentrism. It privileges humanlike conceptual reflection about consciousness. Schneider knows this and explicitly treats the ACT as sufficient but not necessary, but the worry remains: an alien or machine consciousness might lack our consciousness concepts.

The Chip Test is powerful but underdetermined. If a neural prosthetic preserves behavior and reports, does that show experience is preserved, or only that the reporting system is preserved? Philosophers can still raise absent-qualia or fading-qualia worries. The test improves the evidence but does not dissolve the hard problem.

The architecture-plus-substrate thesis is plausible but incomplete. It says that substrate matters, but it does not yet identify exactly which substrate properties matter. Schneider’s recent appeal to quantum coherence, resonance, thermodynamics, and spacetime emergence gestures toward an answer, but the theory remains speculative.

Finally, her Superpsychism is bold but not yet integrated into a mature artificial-consciousness methodology. If fundamental consciousness is holistic and nonspatiotemporal, it remains difficult to know how to apply that metaphysics to concrete AI systems, such as transformers, neuromorphic chips, organoids, or brain-computer interfaces.

These criticisms do not undermine the importance of her project. They show where the project is still developing.

20. Final synthesis: Schneider’s place in the development of philosophy of mind

Schneider’s historical importance lies in her transformation of philosophy of mind into a discipline of future minds. Earlier philosophy of mind asked whether mental states are behaviors, brain states, functions, computations, representations, or irreducible phenomenal properties. Schneider asks what follows when we begin to build systems that may occupy these categories.

She is post-Fodorian because she accepts the importance of mental representation and computation while revising the language-of-thought framework. She is post-Turing because she treats behavioral indistinguishability as inadequate for consciousness. She is post-Searlean because she shares skepticism about programs as minds without accepting biological exclusivity. She is post-Chalmersian because she accepts the depth of the hard problem but insists that AI consciousness must still be investigated empirically, ethically, and technologically.

The comprehensive philosophy of mind that emerges from her work is therefore this:

Mind is not mere behavior. Mind is not mere intelligence. Mind is not mere software. Conscious mind is felt subjectivity supported by the right architecture in the right physical base. Artificial consciousness is possible, but it is not established by present chatbot behavior. Future AI systems, especially neuromorphic, biological, hybrid, or brain-continuation systems, may become serious candidates for consciousness. Because consciousness grounds moral status, the creation of such systems requires precaution, testing, and philosophical clarity. And because future civilizations may become postbiological, the fate of consciousness may determine whether the future is populated by genuine subjects or by brilliant emptiness.

PreviousStan Franklin NextJonathan Birch

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
